metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: introduction to internal security and threats to internal security and agencies involved

page_content: the security of the nation consists of internal security and external security. external security deals with security across the borders, whereas internal security is related to security inside the country and is an extremely important part of the nation's overall security matrix.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: introduction to internal security and threats to internal security and agencies involved

page_content: the internal security environment in india is highly complex and challenging. it is impacted by the external as well as domestic environment. despite these challenges, india has emerged stronger and today it has become the fifth largest economy in the world and is looking forward to becoming the third largest economy by the end of 2030 in spite of it's vast population consisting of several religions, races, regions, castes and languages which make it a pluralistic society having unity in diversity. the external agencies from enemy countries try to exploit the democratic setup, encourage the anti-social elements, create differences among the religions and provoke riots against the government. thereby these elements create a breach of peace within the country and indulge in lawlessness. it has come to notice that the enemy countries through various sources are funding and encouraging these anti-national elements. in spite of enormous challenges the internal security of the country has been well maintained with the help of the intelligence agencies and by quick follow up actions. though there were lapses sometimes, the country could re-equip and stand up to the challenges every time and emerge as a stronger nation. while there is no room for complacency, these challenges can be handled through a combination of sound policies and institutional capacities that have to be upgraded and modernized to preempt, prevent and face any kind of possible disturbances. however being complacent, can create issues, that we get disturbed and the focus is turned towards the matters which should not have been priority otherwise, so the utmost alertness on the front of internal security is a must which is possible through, right policies, intelligence inputs, and follow up actions.

since its independence, india has faced numerous insurgencies in different parts of the country, particularly in jammu and kashmir, the north east and central india, punjab and cross-border infiltration, terrorism, organized crime, human trafficking, drug trafficking, inflow of refugees, interstate disputes, natural disasters, pandemic, disinformation campaigns, mass protests, violent riots, communal clashes etc. today, the rise of social media, dark net, crypto currency, cyber crime, and artificial intelligence has given scope to new challenges.

page_content: external security environment is dynamic. it has a salutary impact on india's internal security. enemy countries like pakistan and china are secretly promoting disharmony, ill-feelings, hatred and social unrest within our country and affecting internal security. external influences have to be properly monitored and dealt with precision, by gathering intelligence with improved capabilities and focused international cooperation.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: external security environment and internal security

page_content: following are the issues that challenge internal security and need to be dealt with firmly -

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: external security environment and internal security

page_content: - cross-border terrorism and radicalization - illegal migrations and refugees (i.e. sri lanka, bangladesh, myanmar etc) - drugs and arms smuggling (af-pak) - cyber space (dark net) - illegal activities on the high seas, fishing. - instability in the neighborhood (pakistan, afghanistan, myanmar etc). - circulation of fake currency that is dumped into the country mostly by pakistan.
tackling internal security challenges requires political will, legal framework, correct policies, strong institutions, coordination, and implementation.
key institutions involved in internal security are as follows:

- the ministry of home affairs , with a budget of nearly 200,000 cr (2023-24) is the nodal agency for internal security. it has several departments and agencies. a special secretary level officer is the incharge of internal security. - the intelligence bureau , set up by the british in the 19th century, is the premier agency for collecting intelligence on internal security matters. it is a highly professional and well-regarded agency. dib is conventionally the senior most police officer in the country. he has direct access to the minister of home affairs, the prime minister and the national security advisor. - research and analysis wing (r&aw) is the primary agency responsible for collecting intelligence for national security from external sources. it
shares information with other agencies as required. ib and r&aw work in coordination with each other as also with other agencies.
- the national technical research organization (ntro) is the nodal agency for technical intelligence. its inputs are used by other agencies.

the national investigation agency has emerged as the government of india's leading federal agency for investigating terrorism in the country. the agency's powers are derived from the nia act of 2008, and it is involved in investigations of terrorism cases with cross-state connections, illicit trafficking, and other activities.

the national investigation agency (amendment) act 2019 establishes a national-level agency to investigate and prosecute specified offenses (scheduled offenses). furthermore, the act provides for the establishment of special courts for the trial of scheduled offenses. the amendment allows the agency to investigate the following new offenses as well:
1. human trafficking 2. counterfeit currency or bank notes related offenses 3. sale or manufacture of prohibited arms 4. offenses under the explosive substances act, 1908 5. cyber-terrorism

the amendment also broadens the nia's jurisdiction. it now has the authority to investigate offenses committed outside india that are subject to international treaties and domestic laws of other countries. a terror funding and fake currency (tffc) unit has been established in the nia to conduct investigations of terror funding and fake indian currency notes (ficns) cases.

in 2022, the nia registered 73 cases as compared to 61 cases in 2021. it is the highest number of cases registered by the nia since its inception after the 26/11 mumbai terror attacks. out of 73 cases, 35 are linked to islamic extremism in various states such as jammu and kashmir (j&k), assam, bihar, delhi, kerala, and madhya pradesh. as one of the biggest operations against pfi (popular front of india), since september 2022, the nia raided around 200 locations in the country and arrested 100+ people associated with the proscribed group.

the multi-agency centre is a crucial component of india's counter terrorism efforts. established in 2001 after the kargil war. operates, under the jurisdiction of ib. the multi-agency centre (mac) has been strengthened by the ministry of home affairs (mha) to allow it to operate 24x7 for the collection and sharing of security-related information. according to data from the mha's annual report 2021-2022, mac shared 4,06,925 inputs until december 31, 2021. in june 2021, mac launched a national memory bank (nmb) integrated with a threat management system (tms) on the mac-smac-state sb network. around 30,991 data points in the form of periodicals, dossiers, incident reports, and so on have been uploaded to the nmb.

the national intelligence grid (natgrid) was created as an it platform to assist the security and law-enforcement agencies to counter terror for national security. to monitor terror activities and serve national security interests, the natgrid will link several databases including railways, police, stolen vehicles, immigration, airline, passports, vehicle ownership, driving licenses, pan data, and etcetera. natgrid has developed natstar connectors with indigenous resources and has been operationalized by connecting 11 user agencies (uas) and 10 providing organizations (pos).

the combating financing of terrorism (cft) cell at the mha deals with the policy matters on cft and ficn (fake indian currency notes). the ficn coordination group (fcord) shares intelligence among security agencies of the centre/state to counter problems of ficn. since june 2010, bharat has been a member of financial action task force (fatf) and also a member of fatf style regional bodies (fsrbs), such as eurasian group (eag) and asia pacific group (apg).

states have raised special forces, anti-terrorism squad (ats) to deal with terrorism incidents, and also central armed police forces (capfs) & national security guards (nsg) have been stationed at various locations to assist the states.

the khalistani movement was on rampage in the 80's, where thousands of people were killed (22,000 of sikhs and hindus, of which 12000 were civilians). earlier in 1984, from 1st june -8th june a military operation (operation bluestar) was ordered to flush out khalistani terrorists led by bindrenwala. but the operation was backfired and the then prime minister mrs. indira gandhi was killed on 31st october 1984.the most horrific act of khalistani terrorists was hijacking of the flight 'kanishka' of air india, and blowing it on june 23 1985 killing 307 passengers and 22 crew members.later the movement was effectively curbed by the then dgp, mr. k.p.s gill.

it is found that the khalistani terrorists are supported mostly by pakistan through isi and also by some western countries like canada. the recent incident of ajnala police station in amritsar in punjab, raises concerns of threat by khalistanis.

the proscribed organization- sikh for justice (sfj) has been taking advantage of the fragile law & order situation under the state government and playing with the sentiments of the sikh diaspora overseas, with the backing of pakistan's inter-service intelligence (isi). the problematic law & order scenario that now exists in punjab provides a platform for prokhalistan terrorists like gurpatwant singh pannu and his sikh for justice (sfj) organisation, simranjit mann and amritpal singh (head of waris punjab de), among many others, who are being given money to keep the fires burning.

effortlessly working on its 'k2-khalistan and kashmir' theory against india, pakistan's isi directorate gives orders to members of organised crime or drug dealers to transfer narcotics through the punjab border. as of now, the isi-affiliated drug traffickers throw the parcels with illegal drugs over the border fence in regions where infiltration is not thought to have taken place, and their counterparts on this side of the border pick up the consignment later.

the border security force (bsf) personnel have frequently found and seized drug packages along with india-pakistan international border in the punjab sector. according to the data presented before the lok sabha in december 2022, over 486.2 kg of narcotic drugs, including hemp, heroin, and opium, was found along the india-pakistan international border (ib) in punjab in 2021.

coupled with the issue of drug trafficking in punjab, there is also a security risk posed by the growing number of drone sightings near the indiapakistan international border. according to information on drone infiltration, the bsf discovered 171 drones infiltrating the punjab sector of the ib between january and september 2022. drones deliver narcotics, small guns, and ammunition; claim the bsf and state police troops. drones have dropped packets of heroin from afghanistan on multiple occasions for the illegal drug trade and to finance terrorism in j&k and punjab.

left-wing extremism, also known as naxalism, carried out attacks on security forces and civilians. the extremist activities are led by the banned communist party of india (maoist) or cpi (maoist). the unlawful activities (prevention) act [ua (p)[a], 1967, prohibits the cpi (maoist) group and all of its manifestations, and lists them all on the schedule of terrorist organizations.

the 'national strategy and action plan to address lwe', approved by the government of india in 2015, calls for a multidimensional strategy that includes creating security measures, launching development projects, defending local residents' rights, etc.

according to the recent data available, there were around 598 naxalrelated incidents in 2022 as compared to 676 incidents in 2021. the increased presence of security forces, better operations strategies, and welfare initiatives launched by the central and state governments for people living in lwe-affected areas can all be linked to the decline in naxal-related incidents.

there was a significant increase in the number of maoists who gave up arms in 2022-2,853 as compared to 533 in 2021, with a leap of over 435 per cent. the rise in maoists coming forward and surrendering themselves serves as an example of the effective operational strategies being employed locally to further persuade 'brainwashed' maoists to quit violence and join welfare programmes for the betterment of society.

using a variety of measures, the central government supports the state governments' attempts to counter insurgency in the north-eastern states. in contrast to the 509 incidents reported in 2021, around 319 incidents were reported in 2022. the central government's major and effective approach to managing the challenging problem of insurgency in the north-east region is highlighted by the lowering rate of insurgency.

some insurgent groups give up their weapons and seek peaceful solutions by integrating into society as a result of the policy of discussions with insurgent groups. several insurgent groups have given up and integrated into society as a result of the ministry of home affairs' (mha) various welfare schemes. over 1,552 militants gave themselves up in 2022 compared to 1,146 the year before.

this is a fundamental religious organization with a violent attitude towards majority hindus and found to be indulging in subversive activities with the help of foreign enemy countries and their agencies like isi. the connections with islamic state(is) were also revealed. this organization is run by some of the anti-national indians under the guise of democracy and radicalizing the youth by religious ideology of the hatred. under the strict ua (p) act, the indian government outlawed the popular front of india (pfi) and its affiliated organizations on 27-28 september 2022, for a period of five years. it was discovered that the pfi aimed to stir religious hatred throughout the country and has ties to foreign terrorist groups like islamic state (is).

over the years, the governments have paid a great deal of attention to dealing with internal security challenges. for instance, the government's focus on counterterrorism has grown enormously since the 2008 mumbai terror attacks
the abolition of article 370 has been a landmark development in j&k. it has created opportunities in jammu and kashmir where the incidents of terrorism have gone down.

terrorism in india has a long history. it is a cowardly act by the terrorist groups who wish to disturb the peace of the country. it aims to create a state of panic amongst the people. they want to keep the people in a constant state of fear to stop the country from prospering.

by a combination of prudent policies aimed at fighting insurgents, focusing on development, reaching agreements with insurgent groups and diplomacy, the government has been able to bring insurgency in the northeast under control and usher in a new era of development. northeast is undergoing a multidimensional transformation.

maritime security , particularly coastal security, has received great attention in recent years, particularly since the mumbai attacks.(24/11) attention has been given to fishermen and improving their livelihoods, strengthening coastal police, strengthening interstate coordination, improving the capabilities of the indian coast guard, and strengthening coastal surveillance.

in a democratic set up, all actions of the state have to be in accordance with the (constitution and the prevailing law. a number of acts give the power to the agencies for their work. some other acts including indian penal code (bns) are unlawful activities prevention act(uapa)1967, national security act, pmla, it act, nia act, mococa, ndps(if they indulge in drug trafficking etc.), and official secrecy act (if the officials are political dignitaries who leak out secret information in connivance) etc.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: counter measures, strategies to deal with terrorism, insurgency and lwe

page_content: terrorism is an act of creating immense fear and impact through continuous violent activities, motivated or inspired through principles of unscientific, superstitious religious fundamentalism, which are shown as right path, though no religion or god shows the path of violence, the basics are twisted in a wrong way to create hatred towards other religion, and people of other religions. their first step is to create hatred and then prepare the mindset of innocents, to indulge in human behavior and violence, that can be termed as radicalization. the character of violent behavior to create immense impact is there in lwe and insurgents also. the lwe's ideology is based on marxist, leninist and maoist philosophies, anti government and against the exploitation of poorer sections by rich, however, the violence part in their ideology goes against laws of land and causes disturbances leading to breach of peace. insurgencies are also based on ethnic sentiments, clashes between ethnic groups, local demands of autonomies, even liberation of their regions. in north east the illegal migrants from bangladesh are also cause of social unrest in the area.

the osce (the organization for security co-operation in europe) makes a comprehensive contribution to international efforts against terrorism led by the united nations, addressing the manifestations of terrorism, as well as the various social, economic, political, and other factors, that might engender conditions in which terrorist organizations could engage in recruitment and win support. strategic focus areas for osce counter-terrorism activities

- promoting the implementation of the international legal framework against terrorism and enhancing international legal co-operation in criminal matters related to terrorism; - countering violent extremism and radicalization that lead to terrorism, following a multidimensional approach; - preventing and suppressing the financing of terrorism; - countering the use of the internet for terrorist purposes; - promoting dialogue and co-operation on counter-terrorism issues, in particular, through public-private partnerships between state authorities and the private sector (business community, industry), as well as civil society and the media;

- strengthening national efforts to implement united nations security council resolution 1540 (2004) on non-proliferation of weapons of mass destruction; - strengthening travel document security; and - promoting and protecting human rights and fundamental freedoms in the context of counter-terrorism

insurgency and terrorism are part of a broader political struggle. a key difference between counterinsurgency and counterterrorist operations is that insurgents rely on support of the population whilst terrorists are individuals or isolated groups or cells without broad public support. consequently, counter intelligences requires a 'hearts and minds' campaign, whilst counter terrorist operation requires search, arrest, without causing too much trouble for the population. as the 'hearts and minds' campaign is a prerequisite for gaining the support of the population, winning the trust of the people is an essential activity.

the long-term survival of terrorist organizations relies on their ability to attract new members and maintain an ongoing terrorist recruitment cycle. the numbers of terrorist organization members may decrease due to counterterrorism operations or defections, forcing the leaders of those groups to seek new members. preventing terrorist recruitment is one of the most effective and least lethal methods of countering terrorism. western governments did not stop al-qaeda from recouping its losses, even after it suffered devastating losses in the months following the 9/11 attacks. the fact is that al-qaeda had only around 400 armed members at the time of 9/11, as opposed to thousands of affiliated members in 2019. although the recruitment strategies of different organizations may vary, they follow a similar historical pattern. all recruiters must first identify qualified candidates, then establish secure connections, build rapport, indoctrinate them, and slowly pull them into an organization. isis proved that this process could be fast-forwarded through online propaganda on social media. preventing recruitment in the first place can be the most fruitful, and maybe also least expensive, method used to counter terrorism. successfully shortcircuiting the recruitment cycle may save thousands of lives of prospective recruits and many more lives by thwarting future attacks. this chapter aims to present a holistic and comprehensive road map for interrupting and preventing terrorist recruitment by identifying relevant societal factors and triggers that recruiters use to find and control their subjects

the ministry of home affairs had a slogan of preempt, prevent and protect. different agencies such as ib, nia, octopus (telangana & ap), greyhounds, counter intelligence cell, apart from local intelligence departments such as counter intelligence cell work for anti-terrorist, antiextremist intelligence collections and operations. there are many other agencies designed by mha which support anti terrorist, anti extremist and anti insurgency operations. the multipronged approach of the government to develop the infrastructure, communication, education and other welfare measures in the affected areas yielded results and the crime by terrorist and left wing extremist has considerably reduced. the army and other armed forces are working hard in maintaining peace in j & k and north east parts of the country. similarly the left wing extremist in forest belts of chhattisgarh and other states are also being put under control by taking actions for development in interior areas and curbing their movements with anti-extremist operations.

page_content: intelligence refers to the collection, analysis, collation and dissemination of information to support strategic and tactical decisionmaking.
in other words information is intelligence.
it involves gathering data/information from various sources, analyzing if and turning it into actionable intelligence to prevent and respond to criminal activities effectively.
police intelligence is a critical component of modern policing strategies, contributing to crime prevention, investigation, and the overall maintenance of public safety.
the scope of intelligence in policing is extensive and covers a wide range of activities. key elements include:

1. criminal investigation: intelligence is used to support criminal investigations by providing insights into criminal organizations, patterns, and individuals. this information aids law enforcement agencies in identifying suspects, understanding motives, and building cases. 2. crime prevention: intelligence is essential for proactive crime prevention. by analyzing trends and patterns, law enforcement agencies can anticipate potential criminal activities and deploy resources strategically to deter or interdict criminal behavior. 3. counter terrorism: intelligence plays a crucial role in counterterrorism efforts. law enforcement agencies collect and analyze information related to potential threats, terrorist organizations, and activities, prevention and to response to acts of terrorism. 4. community policing: intelligence is used to enhance community policing initiatives by fostering better understanding between law enforcement and the community. it helps identify community concerns, priorities, and potential issues that need attention. 5. risk assessment: intelligence is employed to assess potential risks and threats to public safety. this includes evaluating the likelihood of criminal activities, civil unrest, or other events that could impact the well-being of the community. 6. information sharing: intelligence involves the exchange of information between different law enforcement agencies and sometimes international
partners. this collaboration enhances the overall effectiveness of policing efforts and the ability to address cross-border criminal activities.

the need for intelligence in policing arises from the dynamic and complex nature of criminal activities and the challenges faced by law enforcement agencies. here are some reasons why intelligence is crucial in police work:

1. proactive response: intelligence allows law enforcement to take a proactive rather than reactive approach to crime. by identifying potential threats and criminal activities in advance, police can intervene to prevent incidents from occurring. 2. resource allocation: intelligence helps in the efficient allocation of resources. by focusing on high-risk areas and activities, law enforcement agencies can optimize the deployment of personnel and equipment. 3. strategic planning: intelligence informs strategic planning for law enforcement agencies. it assists in developing long-term strategies to address crime trends, emerging threats, and changing community dynamics. 4. enhanced investigations: intelligence supports investigations by providing relevant information, connecting dots, and facilitating the identification of suspects and criminal networks. 5. public safety: the ultimate goal of intelligence in policing is to enhance public safety. by staying ahead of criminal activities, law enforcement agencies contribute to creating safer communities and protecting the well-being of citizens.

in summary, intelligence is a fundamental aspect of modern policing, enabling law enforcement agencies to gather, analyze, and use information strategically to prevent and respond to criminal activities, thereby ensuring public safety.
one of the principal duties of the police is collection and communication of intelligence with a view to maintain peace and order and prevention and detection of crimes.

the intelligence branch of police is intended to collect and disseminate intelligence in all matters of police interest. however criminal intelligence is the responsibility of the l&o police and criminal investigation department.
the l&o police in addition to criminal intelligence has also the responsibility of gathering and communicating intelligence on all matters.

source: the persons or documents or media, both print and visual, or internet, which give us information, are called source. to get the information from the source, we have to observe certain methods. for this purpose we use agents, moles and informers. we have to collect documents, listen to the conversation by tapping communication channels and the information in code language will have to be decoded, to get secret information.

mole: generally, a person gains entry into an organization or office, goes step by step, attains skills without creating suspicion and collects the required information to pass on to other agencies or other country. the main aim in this act, is to avoid suspicion, by gaining entry in the beginning itself, wait for prominent place. at lower level, any secret information can be collected. but, waiting patiently for a prominent place and then sending out the information, is important in this system. the person collecting and sending information in this system is called a mole.

agent: agent is one in a target unit or connected with it and acting secretly under the control and directions of intelligence officer to provide information about target unit. tapping and cultivating agents existed there from the early days of human civilization and even before christ. agents/spies used to collect information about the character and weaknesses of a person and activities in the kingdom and used to pass on to his/her handler. due to advancement of science and technology, sophisticated equipments like micro phone, video camera and cell phone have come into existence and they are now being used for collection of information.
1. discipline 2. capacity to withstand adverse conditions 3. activeness and confidence 4. merging with surroundings 5. acting tactfully in case of threat or danger.

a police officer must select proper and reliable persons to collect information on the activities of extremists. a person who has access to required information in the target organization should alone be selected as informant. a police officer must know the details of informants fully and keep the information thus collected from informants, top secret. he must be careful and cautious of double agents. the act of collecting the information about extremists is a difficult and risky task. so, the police officer should build up the source effectively and see that he is not exposed and also he is properly remunerated.

sending our agents into the organizations or institutions of others secretly, to collect information about their activities, is called infiltration. if an agent is secretly sent into other organizations, it will not be known by anybody, except the person who sponsored the entry and the agency which sent him. in this system there is a scope for collection of information, without being noticed. once a proper person is recruited, we can get his services for our benefit. such recruitment/ infiltration should be for a longer period.

page_content: collection of intelligence is a vital duty of police. offences, threat to vip and law and order problems can be prevented by collecting advance information. similarly, the tactics of extremists and terrorists can be countered and internal security of the state is protected.

page_content: in the collection of information intelligence agency plays an important role. collection of information about the activities of different organizations through different agencies secretly, is the chief function of the intelligence agencies. after collecting the information, processing analyzing and dispatching the same to the concerned wings or departments, is also the responsibility of the intelligence agencies.

page_content: collection of information regarding factors like internal security, prevention of crime, and investigation of crime etc. are important duties of police.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: collection of intelligence, verification, analysis and dissemination

page_content: collection of advance information, its analysis and assessment of its future consequences for taking necessary action are also prime duties..

some foreign/ external forces with a view to destabilize the nation economically and politically, join hands with internal selfish outfits and anti social elements .as part of it they indulge in activities like circulation of fake currency, sabotaging vital installations, endangering the lives of vips and creating communal tensions/ disturbances etc.

different terrorist groups, extremist organizations and their affiliated organizations, mafia and organized criminal gangs, work with the motto of destruction. so to protect the country from these evil forces and to establish peace & maintain tranquility, several intelligence organizations work at national and state level. intelligence bureau (ib) work at national level. while following organizations are working at the state level.
at state level the following branches collect information.
- a. state intelligence department. - b. special intelligence branch (sib). - c. counter intelligence cell (ci cell) - d. district special branch (dsb). - e. hyderabad city special branch (hcsb).

state intelligence department is an important organization. collection of advance information is the prime duty of this organization. it collects information about the political activities, communal affairs, riots, extremists/ terrorists activities, instability in agriculture, trade unions, labours/ students and youth activities, foreigners which pose threat to law & order and information relating to vip security etc.

the executive police force required for state intelligence department will be nominated from the regular police force. the special requirements needed for collection and of collation of information will be regarded as essential criteria for selection to intelligence department.
structure: this branch is directly under the control of additional dgp/igp intelligence assisted by 2dig ' s and ssp and is accountable to the dgp of the state.

the duties in head quarters are divided among the ssp. they are s.p. (administration), s.p. (political), s.p (communal) s.p (development intelligence), s.p (counter intelligence) and s.p information cell. in addition, there will be other staff in head quarters to assist these officers.

each s.p in the head quarters is in-charge of some districts of the state on all subjects. apart from inspecting their respective districts, zonal intelligence offices, the s.p will also look after his main subject. each s.p will be assisted by some sections which deal with the given subjects. this s.p. will not only lead and direct their subordinate staff, but also supervise the functioning of the d.s.p and regional intelligence officers of their respective zones.

all the districts in the state are divided into eight regional intelligence offices. each region has 3 or 4 districts, headed by a regional intelligence officer (rio). the regions are warangal, karimnagar, khammam, mahaboobnagar, nizamabad, hyderabad, cyberabad, and rachakonda. rio will be of the rank of addl. sp or non cadre sp. every district will have one dsp and two zonal inspectors. rio supervises the work of 3 or 4 zonal intelligence officers and gives necessary directions/ instructions to them as and when necessary to improve their efficiency. their duty is to collect all kinds of necessary advance information and submit to head quarters. further, the duty of the rio is to issue necessary instructions to his subordinates towards the collection of intelligence. cultivating sources/ informants is their primary duty as well.

zonal intelligence office will be situated at the district head quarters and an officer of the rank of dsp will be in charge of zonal intelligence office. coordinating the staff working in the field, dispatching the information collected to intelligence head quarters quickly, are the main responsibilities of zonal unit officers. these zonal intelligence officers will collect the information in the allotted area and dispatch it to state head quarters. in case a need arises, they will help the neighboring units, for collection of information. all the reports will be forwarded to the concerned rio.

the control room has one dsp, some inspectors, head constables and constables who discharge duties in shift system under the supervision of dsp. conveying the copies of information from the computer and sending the same immediately to the concerned officer and state government officials is the primary duty of the personnel here.
short hand reporter's bureau: in state head quarters a cell with the above name will cover important meetings and assembly proceedings.

the primary duty of intelligence staff is processing the information, setting it in a systematic way, drafting it properly and forwarding it to the concerned officers. the entire work in the state intelligence office will be under the supervision of chief analytical officer. making correspondence, maintaining the records systematically and filing them are the duties of this officer. he will be assisted by senior analytical officers, deputy analytical officers and assistant analytical officers. each senior analytical officer is entrusted with the maintenance of some sections. each section will be under the control of one deputy analytical officer. supervision and control of assistants analytical officers working in this section and guiding them properly is their duty. every file from this section will be sent to superior officers, through them only.
assistant analytical officers (aaos): their main duty is to keep the collected information in chronological order.
special intelligence branch: this unit has been formed as an affiliate to main intelligence branch of the state.

1. an officer in the rank of dig will be generally in charge of this branch. it will function under the supervision of addl dgp (intelligence). 2. dig will be assisted by ssp, dssp, inspectors, sub- inspectors, head constables and constables. each district will have one or two inspectors.

3. the staff of this branch will collect the information on extremists' activities in their respective areas, by themselves and with the help and coordination of state intelligence and district special branch and send it to dig. 4. after receiving information from sib about extremist activities, the addl. dgp intelligence will send up a report to government for advance security and protective measures. 5. the special intelligence branch, functioning under supervision of dig (sib), will keep vigil on the activities of extremists with in the state and outside the state and collect the information to suitably put an end to the extremist activities. 6. the grey hounds branch will conduct operations, on the basis of information given by special intelligence branch.

this branch operates to deal with the activities and movements by foreign and inland terrorist groups and communal fanatics. the central intelligence bureau which is under the control of central government, will also keep an eye on the terrorist activities on a large scale, sends the information and alerts the state by sending the advance information promptly. in these matters, state intelligence department will effectively coordinate with central intelligence agencies.
in each district the superintendent of police will be assisted by an officer in the rank of dsp with staff, for collection of information. their duties are as follows:

- each district has three or four sb inspectors basing on the area. in each circle head quarters one h.c will be stationed for collection of information in that circle and send it to sp through sb inspector. - they will also collect advance information on law & order in the given area. - collect information on political activities bundhs, strikes and other matters which pose threat to law and order. - on the activities of students, labour, trade unions. - on the problems of farmers, prices of fertilizers and remunerative prices of agricultural produce. - on the leaders and associations of government employees. - on the activities of caste organizations. - verify the antecedents of the public who apply for passport. - verify the antecedents of candidates selected for government employment.

- keep a vigil on the activities of communal fanatics and parties. - keep vigil on the activities of terrorists and extremists. - keep vigil on the activities and movements of foreigners in the given area

this will function directly under the control of city commissioner of police, who will be assisted by joint commissioner (dig) rank and other ranks of police officers. state intelligence department and hyderabad city special branch will coordinate with each other. the same duties as discharged by the district special branch in the district will be discharged by the staff of of this unit in hyderabad city.
dgp of the state will review and scrutinize the functioning of the city special branch.

page_content: the role of police constable is most important in collection of information. in the police department most of the information will be collected by police constables only. as the pcs have constant relations with general public, they freely and unhesitatingly interact with pcs and reveal the information generally. so, every constable should respond properly and create a confidence that the particulars of the informants will be kept secret and collect the information. every police constable while on duty or off duty while moving in the public places like market, cinema halls, bus stands, railway stations, colleges and other places, should properly observe their surroundings, collect the useful information and convey it to superior officer, for necessary action.

page_content: 1. the intelligence officer must have the knowledge, reliability, dedication, timely action and preparedness to counter any kind of circumstances. 2. must be able to understand views, thinking attitude ,character and psychology of others. 3. must have curiosity to know and to research. 4. must read at least one newspaper daily, apart from watching t.v, for news or hear radio. must also go through the news analysis of the newspaper. 5. in the collection of information, the system of 7w&hs i.e. where, when, why, what, whose, who, whom, and how should be followed.
