page_content: as a model, standard operative procedure for investigation in the case of murder is given below:

generally, the term 'homicide' means the killing of a human being by another human being. all homicides are not crimes, certain homicides committed under self-defence are not criminal acts or unlawful homicides. as per ipc or by any other special laws only unlawful homicides (murders) are punishable.
section 302 ipc. punishment for murder : whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

- -land disputes - -family disputes - -long pending previous enmities - -political supremacy - -faction - -financial matters - -mafia gangs - -communal - -hired murders (supari killings) - -boundary disputes in agricultural fields. - -irrigation disputes

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: when complainant/ petitioner comes to police station to lodge the complaint

page_content: offer chair to the petitioner and make him comfortable which would repose confidence in him -

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: if the complainant brought written complaint/ petition

page_content: - -take petition, go through the contents - -enquire with him about the contents to confirm the facts

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: if the complainant want to report matter orally

page_content: - -offer him a white paper to write and obtain the same in writing under the signature of the complainant.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: if the complainant want to report matter orally

page_content: - -if he cannot write the petition, the sho by himself or through his subordinate shall reduce the oral statement of complainant into writing, read over the statement and take his signature after he admitting the contents are correct.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: in both the instances (a&b) mentioned above

page_content: - -make endorsement on the complaint mentioning - date and time of receipt of complaint/ statement - crime number - sections of law of ipc, special and local laws as per the contents of the petition. - -make an entry in the general diary - -register the case by issuing fir - -provide a copy of fir free of cost to complainant (sec. 154 (2) cr. p.c.) - -dispatch original fir to jurisdictional magistrate forthwith - -inform about offence to concerned officers. - -i.o. proceeds to scene of crime with io kit box and case file.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: soon after the receipt of the complaint -

page_content: - -depute two staff members to soc with required material of guarding scene (cones and scene cordoning tape/ ribbon) to preserve the evidence. instruct them to control the curious on-lookers who may disturb scene of offence and clear unauthorized persons. - -if required make a request for utilizing the services of- - clues team - dog squad in charge officer.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: investigation process at scene of crime (soc).

page_content: secure the presence two respectable and independent mediators (panchas) of the locality.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offence

page_content: - -note the time of arrival at scene of offence. - -note weather conditions, especially at outdoor crime scenes. - -check perimeter of scene to ensure that entire scene is secured. - -note down the gps coordinates (latitudes-longitudes) of the scene of crime. - -clear unauthorized person from the scene. - -prevent anyone from touching the dead body or disturbing anything in the entire scene of offence

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: investigation activity like survey and recording of scene of offence

page_content: - -when it is outdoor scene of offence, identify and fix the permanent objects/ reference points. - -do not question the victims hastily to know the details of offence- - allow the victim to stabilize. - ease tension of the victims by consoling them. - then examine them about the details of the offence - -at night - obtain adequate lighting before commencement of processing of scene. artificial lighting used must be adequate for taking photography and for searching and identifying minute detail for items like hair, cartridge cases, etc. - -when lighting not available, secure scene under guard and wait for daylight before processing is commenced. - -reconstruction of scene with the help of eyewitnesses - -place compass to know magnetic north - -enter scene by route least likely to disturb evidence, noting route of travel. - -if the scene of offence is very large organise scene search by adopting specific plan, assign tasks, areas of search to individual officers. - -examine the scene of offence in the presence of mediators minutely by following standard methods for identifying physical evidence/ material objects (m.os.) like weapons of offense, blood stains which are useful for investigation. - -provide numbers to each identified material object (m.o.) so that they are clearly visible in photographs and easy to explain about such m.os. in observation report (panchanama) and sketch map of scene of crime - -take measurements of the scene carefully with specific reference to permanent object/ reference point, material objects and other important features of the crime scene.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: make careful note of the following points in respect of dead body:

page_content: - position of dead body - physical condition of dead body and clothing - location of any injuries on deceased and his clothing - ascertain any alteration of position of dead body after the offence before arrival investigating officer (io) - before shifting dead body examine deceased for physical evidence example: loose hairs, fibre, etc. - move the dead body to shortest possible distance and place it on a mat - examine the ground underneath the victim

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: make careful note of the following points in respect of dead body:

page_content: - examine entire dead body (back and front side) for additional physical evidence that may become visible after movement.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: take digital photographs of scene on following lines:

page_content: - cover all the material objects and reference points (longshot), - cover all the material objects identified including the dead body - each material object shall be photographed separately (close shots)

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: collecting material objects and physical evidence

page_content: - -collect material objects/ physical evidence which are useful in the investigation like weapons of offense, blood stains and pack carefully without causing any damage. - -collect required physical evidence from the deceased person like clothing etc. - -the process of packing, labelling, and sealing attestation by punch witnesses should be completed at the scene of crime itself.

- -carefully fill up all the columns of crime detail form - -with reference to column numbers 9 and 10 of cdf, it is better to prepare a mediators report (observation report/ panchanama) of scene of offence separately and attach it to cdf. - -such mediators report shall be prepared in the presence of mediators by describing the complete details whatever is done at scene of offence including the description and collection of physical evidence, property recovered/ seized for the purpose of investigation because they are the witnesses who depose evidence about entire process done at scene of crime in the court. the date and time of starting and closing shall be mentioned in mediators report. - -after recording mediators report, the io shall read over the contents to mediators and obtain their signatures when they agree that the contents are true.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: draw sketch map of scene of crime as per the specifications.

page_content: with reference to column numbers 11 of cdf, it is better to prepare a sketch map of scene of offence separately and attach it to cdf.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: conducting inquest over the dead body and sending dead body for conducting pme:

page_content: - -conducting inquest over the dead body of the deceased in the presence of mediators by carefully filling each and every column of inquest proforma accurately - -describe general description of the dead body.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: conducting inquest over the dead body and sending dead body for conducting pme:

page_content: - -describe the direction, physical position, and condition of the dead body - -mention clothing (style, colour, unusual appearances such as tears, holes, pockets turned inside - outside etc) any stains. - -describe location and appearance of wounds, bruises, foreign materials etc. - -note the details about the blood: (a) location (b) pattern (c) direction of flow (d) degree of coagulation on body and clothes. - -describe condition, location and extent of (1) rigor mortis (2) hypostasis other body conditions. - -every detail touching the apparent cause of the death shall be recorded. - -exact measurements must be given, and vague expressions should be avoided. - -after completing writing of inquest report - -write requisition to medical officer for conducting postmortem examination over the dead body of the deceased. - -shifting the dead body of deceased to mortuary. - -make one officer responsible for collecting seized material objects for safe custody. - -dispatch the original inquest to jurisdictional magistrate.

- -medical officer shall conduct postmortem examination on the dead body of deceased. - -collection and preserving of viscera of deceased in bottles by medical officer if required. - -collection of such viscera bottles from medical officer for sending to fsl - -obtain short opinion of mo immediately

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: activity in police station after returning from scene of crime

page_content: - -entering the details of material objects in the 'property seizure register' - -ensuring the chain of custody perfectly when material objects are further handled for sending same to fsl etc. by taking signatures of the persons handling. - -send all the material objects to forensic science laboratory (fsl) and other experts.
- -recording of statements of all the witnesses like eyewitnesses, victims, injured persons, u/s 161 cr. p. c.

- -recording of statements of circumstantial witnesses including witnesses who speaks about the motive preparation part of offence (sec. 8 of iea) - -witnesses if any who speaks about the previous and subsequent conduct of accused (sec. 8 of iea) - -write first case diary (section 172 cr. p. c. record of investigation) incorporating entire process by mentioning the timings and places visited including the further plan of action. - -obtain detail fsl reports, pme report, other expert reports if any.

- -arrest the accused person(s) by observing required formalities (sections 41-b, 46, 50, cr. p. c) - -intimate about the arrest to the friend or relative nominated by accused as per section 50-a cr. p. c and serve a notice accordingly. - -prepare arrest memo (iif - iii) by filling all columns carefully and properly. - -the arrested persons should be produced before the medical officer with a requisition for examination as required u/s 53 cr. p. c. - -entering the details of accused persons in the records such as prisoner search register, sentry relief book, general diary. - -interrogate accused persons and record detailed interrogation report about his involvement in the case and to know his background and previous criminal history. - -the version of the accused should be verified carefully and thoroughly. additional information if any obtained during such verification can be further probed into.

- -arrested person should be produced before the magistrate within 24 hours of his arrest with a detailed remand case diary, explaining circumstances leading to incident and linking the criminal to crime, evidence collected, details of recovery if any and motive for the offence. - -the previous involvement of the accused in such cases if any should be mentioned in remand diary along with disposal particulars. - -the accused will be remanded to judicial custody only when the investigation discloses prima facie evidence against him and to complete further investigation. - -when the investigation is not completed and where the presence of accused is required to know the certain facts of the case, the accused can be taken for police custody by filing a requisition before the magistrate.

- -if the investigation is not completed within the period of judicial remand, requisition has to be filed in the court for extension of remand period. - -whenever bail petition is filed in the court by the accused persons oppose the bail petition with sufficient grounds.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: 8 . review of evidence by i.o. and finalisation of investigation of case

page_content: - -the io shall review his own investigation and critically analyze the efforts made and evidence collected. if necessary, collect further evidence and confirm that entire evidence collected will prove all the ingredients of sections applied. - -planning for sequencing of evidence for proper presentation in the court. - -ensure that the expert's reports are not against the theory of prosecution. - -the io should not hesitate to seek guidance/ additional assistance from the supervisory officers and legal officers in complicated cases. if he suggests for collection of further oral/ documentary evidence to strengthen the case. such instructions shall be carried out before finalization of investigation case.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: preparing of charge sheet (sec. 173 cr. p. c.) and filing in the court

page_content: - -after completion of the investigation, charge sheet proforma {final report form (iif-v)} containing 20 columns are to be filled up properly. - -the charge sheet (which is to be enclosed to column number 16 of proforma) is to be prepared carefully covering all points in chronological order. - -particulars of witnesses to be examined (memorandum of evidence) (list of witnesses), type of evidence to be tendered is to be prepared the proforma shown in column number 13 - -in column number 13 of proforma, witnesses to be examined (memorandum of evidence), evidence to be tendered etc. are provided. all the columns should be filled accurately. - -all the documents to be proved by the prosecution are to be listed separately. - -if all the accused persons charge sheeted are not arrested by the time of filing charge sheet, the court may be requested to issue nbws against absconding accused persons. - -prepare and serve notice on the complainant u/s 173 (2) (ii) cr. p. c. - -while filing charge sheet in court, see that all the required documents, case property, material objects returned from fsl are deposited in the court. - -the copies of the charge sheet in full shape to be furnished to the prosecuting officer.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: preparing of charge sheet (sec. 173 cr. p. c.) and filing in the court

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: definition of terrorism, origin and historical perspective:-

page_content: frightening and terrifying the people through immense acts of violence, and indulging in torture of targeted people or innocent civilians and damaging the public and private properties in order to achieve the vicious goals which may be political, non-political or ideological is 'terrorism'.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: definition of terrorism, origin and historical perspective:-

page_content: as per the united nations security council, in its resolution 1566 of october 2004, elaborates this definition, stating that terrorists acts are 'criminal acts, including against civilians, committed with the intent to cause death or serious bodily injury, or taking of hostages, with the purpose to provoke a state of terror in the general public or in a group of persons or particular persons, intimidate a population or compel a government or an international organisation to do or to abstain from doing any act.'

page_content: - a. religious fundamentalism: majority of the terrorist groups in india, have been formed on the basis of religious fundamentalism. islamic terrorism is a problem not only to india, but the entire world. some neighboring countries have made religious fundamentalism as a syllabus, in educational institutions and converting the youth as terrorists. there is extreme urgency to attend to this issue, with humanitarianism, taking measures of anti terror anti fundamental organizations, which are producers of terrorists. the basic thing is to educate the people for patriotism rather than radicalism, which can act as a deterrent for terrorism. stern action need to be taken against, radicalizing agencies, and people who silently radicalize the people. 2. b . economic disparities and unemployment: -world wide survey indicates that extremist agencies are more in number in the developing and underdeveloped countries, than in the developed countries. unemployment problem increases due to lack of economic development, resulting in the poor people getting attracted towards terrorism and antisocial activities. - c. illiteracy: - illiteracy plays an important role in the growth of terrorism and creates multifaceted problems. people will be unable to understand their social conditions and their minds are open to persons who instigate them, particularly they are likely to be misguided by fundamental organizations, who radicalize them on the basis of religion.. due to lack of understanding capacity to go on to the path of development, illiterates, either directly or indirectly extend their cooperation to extremists, in interior most forest areas where there is influence from left wing

terrorism in punjab:punjab, which is the north west state of india, is one of the most fertile and richest states. in the history of post independence period, punjab was a hub of bloodshed struggles of terrorism, for nearly a decade. the reason was the struggle for power between the sikh political leaders and government of india.

in the beginning of 1980, the discussions held with the central government, under the leadership of sant bhindranwale, for autonomy of punjab state, were not fruitful, the sikhs chose the path of violence. because of terrorism by sikhs in 1980, late smt. indira gandhi dissolved the legislative assembly then ruled by akalidal. consequently, the akali dal party came to power in elections with a marginal majority. from that day onwards, the issue began. sikh terrorists started anti governmental activities.

in september 1981, a prominent hindu editor was killed and the followers of bhindranwale were suspected. bindranwale surrendered to the police and was released on bail after a month. subsequent to this incident, the sikhs increased attacks on general public. the government of late smt. indira gandhi started discussions with akali dal party about the political future of punjab state. akali dal party demanded autonomy to punjab state and large share in the local funds to chandigarh and other regions where punjabis are more in number. but, this demand was opposed by the neighbouring haryana state, as it was dominated by hindu population. hence, the then central government in may 1982, stalled the discussions, without any solution on the demands of akali dal. various sikh terrorists groups were banned by the central government. as a consequence, all the members of these organizations selected the golden temple of amritsar as their hideout. bindranwale also joined them, making the golden temple, an armoury and its headquarters. as the discussions for political solution were not fruitful, other sikh political groups also joined hands with terrorists.

in the year 1984, president ' s rule was imposed in punjab state, to curtail the activities of terrorists. as a result, serious violations of human rights took place. illegal detentions and harassments became common. on june 3rd of 1984 the sikh leaders gave a call for fresh struggle. as a countermeasure, the then prime minister
late smt. indira gandhi sent an army and imposed curfew in punjab, canceling all emergency services.

on june 4th 1984, as part of the maintenance of law and order, indian army attacked the golden temple with the name of operation 'blue star '', in which sikh terrorists including bindranwale lost their lives. in the same year on october 31st the then prime minister late smt. indira gandhi was shot dead, by her sikh bodyguards. nearly until the year 1992 the sikh militants had formed into seven large groups and 12 small groups with secession as their aim, making the punjab a blast furnace for a decade. the effective steps undertaken by the then dgp of punjab sri.kps gill along with the indian army considerably eliminated the sikh terrorism. the violence started coming down from 1990 onwards. on february 19th of 1992 elections were conducted to the assembly and democracy was restored in punjab.

the kashmir terrorism: the kashmir valley, jammu area and low lying area of ladakh are within the indian territory, and the pak occupied kashmir and gilgit baltistan, and some areas of tribals lie towards the border of pakistan. when india became independent, in 1947 and bifurcated into two countries, kashmir was a province which was autonomous and independent.. at that time, pakistan attacked kashmir, by sending an army in the guise of tribals, in 1948,and occupied about one third of kashmir, and gilgit baltistan and the then king of kashmir, maharaja hari singh signed an instrument of accession of kashmir in favor of india,, and indian army entered into picture, and did not allow further occupation of kashmir by pakistan. the kashmir with srinagar valley, jammu and ladakh were retained by india the pakistan claims. due to this kashmir problem, wars have taken place between india and pakistan twice, i.e. in the years 1948 and 1965.in another war of 1971 ,the separation of bangladesh from pakistan, occurred, both the countries entered into an agreement in the year 1972 (simla agreement) and arrived at an understanding to solve the problem mutually..the loc was drawn and pakistan, was not supposed to cross the borders. but they were, always trying to create troubles by sending terrorist outfits into our country and have been provoking the youth to indulge in violence.in 1990s the terror incidents in kashmir were at peak, and many hindus were killed, and lakhs of kashmiri pandits were driven out from their properties, and were forced to be refugees in their own country. as a result armed forces, and military was deployed, and that has resulted in human rights violation. however, in the recent years, the measures taken for jammu and kashmir development, have yielded results, now it is by and large peaceful, recently in the year 2023, the g20 summit was also organized here, where india could showcase the coming back of normalcy in kashmir.

the government of india re-constituted the former state of jammu and kashmir into two union territories, jammu and kashmir, ladakh, in october 2019,and the article 370, which gives special status was scrapped,. there by entire jammu and kashmir will be having the same status as the other states, with in india. after this kashmir has returned to peace, and normalcy, violence has come down drastically.

on september 11th of 2001, a terrorist group by name al-qaeda, under the leadership of osama-bin-laden of saudi arabia attacked the world trade center (wtc) in america. after this incident, all the democratic and secular countries of the world have recognized the seriousness of terrorism and started efforts to eliminate it. india also has taken many steps in this direction.

- a. irish republican army (ira)was established as a special military organization from irish volunteers which was started on november 25th of the year 1913. this outfit used to resort to terrorist activities, to become independent from the rule of england. at present, there is an agreement between ira and the government of england for cease fire, which was entered into in the year 1997 and thus, the violence was put to an end. on 8-5-2007, ian pisely and martin magnise, were sworn in as the first minister and deputy minister, respectively. at present, peace is prevailing.

- b. euzicady-ta-ascatusuna (e.t.a)this organization was started in some areas of france (sw) n spain, basing on the marxist policies, to establish independent kingdoms. its full name is euzicady-ta-ascatusuna. this is also called basque father land and liberty organization. it was trained by libya and attacking the security forces of spain and its government. - c. red armyits full name is japanese red army. it was established in the year 1970. its main aim was dethroning the king in japan and it conducted attacks worldwide, against imperialism. it has its existence in syria and lebanon. the army operating in japan is called red army. another one which is operating against worldwide imperialism, is called united red army. it has links with 'popular front for the liberation of palestine'. at present it is not active. - d. chechnya rebelschechnya is a small state, located in the southern side of russia. between 1900-14, the labour of oil refinery of russia have been sent to grozni, the capital of chechnya and other areas. the nationals of chechnya have tried to protect their own land, from 1905 onwards. until 1991, there was no rebel of any sort. in 1991, when russia fragmented, chechnyans have also tried to get separated. in the year 1994, when there was internal war in chechnya, russia sent 40 thousand security forces, but was not successful. when there was revolt again in the year 1999, it was brutally suppressed.

1. ltteliberation tigers of tamil eelam. it was established in the year 1976 by velu pillai prabhakaran, in sri lanka. its main aim was to establish tamil state in the north east area of sri lanka. this is popular for conducting attacks using suicide bombers. the government of norway made efforts to establish peace between sri lanka and ltte. after the srilankan military defeated tamil tigers and killed v. prabhakaran, bringing the civil war to an end in the may 2009. 2. al-qaedathis organization was established by osama-bin-laden in the later part of 1980. its present, its present headquarters is in afghanistan. its main aim is to establish muslim kingdom in the world. in the year 2001, two buildings of world trade centre in america were blown, by them which is treated as a major and serious attack in the history of islamic terrorism. it also extends cooperation to worldwide muslim terrorist groups. 3. talibansthis is a terrorist organization, which is working to establish a muslim rule in afghanistan. even though the american government dethroned the talibans after wto attack, talibans are making attacks on the government, with the help of al-qaida. the taliban captured afghanistan in 2021 after the us military withdrew and formed a government.

4. hamasthis organization was started in 1987 with the aim of establishing islamic palestine rule, in the place of israel. it recieves funds from iran, non- migrants of palestine and from some richest muslims of saudi arabia. it is carrying out attacks against israeli forces. 5. hizbullahthis organization was started in lebanon, to achieve political power. it is getting weapons and training from iran and syria. it is also opposing israel and creating hurdle in peace talks in the middle east.

- a. lashkar-e-taiba (let): started in the year 1989, with an aim of merging kashmir in pakistan. it has training camps in muzaffarabad of pakistan and muridke near lahore. it carries out attacks not only in kashmir, but also in different places of india. - b. jaish-e-mohammed (jem): started by moulana masood azhar in february 2000, with the aim of merging kashmir in pakistan. it has training camps in peshawar and muzaffarabad in pakistan and also in afghanistan. it has links with talibans and osama-bin-laden. it also carries attacks in different parts of india. - c. harkat-ul-jihadi-islami (huji): started in the year 1980 in afghanistan against soviet troops. its branch was established in bangladesh, in the year 1992. shaukath osman alias shaik fareed is its leader. as per intelligence report, its members reside in the old city of hyderabad. its main aim is to establish islamic rule in bangladesh. it is also carrying out terrorist attacks in india. these people are also called bangladesh talibans.

ulfa: its full name is united liberation front of assam. it was started on april 7, 1979. mr. paresh baruwa is the commander in chief of the military wing of this organization. its aim is to establish sovereign and democratic assam through armed struggle. as per intelligence reports, its camps are located in bangladesh, bhutan and myanmar. isi of pakistan and terrorist groups of bangladesh are extending their cooperation to this organization.

recruitment in terrorist organizations: in india, terrorist organizations are promoting differences among various communities and sowing seeds of hatred in them. students are being recruited by terrorist organizations, brain washing them at educational institutions, by stating that their religion is being discriminated against apart from students, even experts of it field and small businessmen, are also being targeted for recruitment.

terrorist organizations have a broad network, with their branches in india and in almost all the countries. if the leaders of these organisations issue instructions, its members in other countries, procure explosives, weapons, finances and communication systems (internet), necessary for an attack.

terrorists sleeper cells:these cells are formed to carryout clandestine activities, without getting noticed. as these groups are very small, they can easily mix up with public and it is very difficult to collect information in detail, about their attacks. if the leader of the cell is apprehended in advance, then only there is scope to neutralize the plan of attack.

according to oxford dictionary, sleeper cell is a cell affiliated to terrorist organization, acts as per its direction and carries out attacks. its members will mix up with the general public and lead a normal life without creating suspicion, wait for an opportunity and then carry out terrorist attacks. these cells are useful to the organization, by being in small groups and carryout the activities secretly. each cell contains 3 to 5 members.

proliferation of arms and ammunition: on 13-6-2005, syed salauddin, supreme commander of hijb-ul-mujahideen organization, was arrested and he informed that his organization was training its members in jammu and kashmir, in using small weapons.

illegal weapons and grenades have entered into kashmir, between 1950-60. in 1980-90 weapons have been smuggled into jammu and kashmir on a large scale. isi played an important role in supplying arms to terrorist organizations.

use of explosives and ieds: the terrorist organizations use them to carry out attacks and cause heavy loss of life and property. explosives are called area weapons, because they cause heavy loss to persons and property in a particular area.

eg: rdx. its full name is research development explosive. this is a high explosive. its vod (velocity on detonation) is between 3000m/s and 9000m/s. this is a powerful explosive. terrorists use this, in majority of their attacks.

improvised explosive device (ied): the designed objects, in the shape of various articles, filled with explosives, to be used in the attack, against the target, without getting noticed, are called ieds. these are two types.
1. open ied 2. closed ied.

financial resources: to carry out attacks, terrorist organizations require financial backing. weapons are being smuggled from neighboring countries. fake currency is also put into circulation, to distort the economic conditions of the country. they are investing in stock markets also, for financial resources and utilizing the profit, for terrorist activities.

hawala transactions- mafia gang connections- and their involvement in terrorism: bringing foreign exchange into our country and sending our currency to foreign countries by evading tax payment and violation of foreign exchange management act (fema)-1999 on a large scale, is called hawala. in our country, this hawala business has a large network. sometimes, these hawala transactions are resulting in murders also. circulation of fake currency is also taking place. strict measures are being initiated to control this hawala business.
in 1993 explosions, in bombay, transport of explosives, supply of illicit weapons and planting of bombs, were done by mafia gangs.

supari killers -syndicates of kidnapping and extortions - hired assassins: supari killers: by remaining in different countries, these persons nominate men, kidnap people, demand large amount, for release with an ultimatum, that the kidnapped people will be killed, if the random money is not paid. they do not hesitate to murder the kidnapped people. supari killers form as a syndicate and carry out this kind of unlawful activity. hired killers who escape in novel ways, after committing murders for money, are called supari killers. mafia gangs of mumbai are having these people as members and committing offences.

terrorism is assuming different dimensions, day by day. this activity, called a proxy war, is aimed at destabilizing the country ' s political, financial and social sectors. the following steps have to be taken, to put an end to their activities.

1. satellites etc., with sophisticated technology, have been put into use, to prevent the attacks in the borders, airports and through sea (ports). 2. information on various terrorist organizations in the country and their frontal organizations and their activities can be collected and given to all the state governments. 3. in crowded places and shopping malls, installation of dfmds, cc cameras and internal checking, at all the building complexes, their periodical inspection will help to prevent terrorist attacks. 4. all ranks of officers in the police department must have proper understanding of the activities of terrorists, taking place in their jurisdiction.

counter intelligence: deactivating the enemy intelligence groups, by getting advance information about their activities, is called counter intelligence. all the activities undertaken by the government, to collect information about enemy intelligence groups, can be termed as counter intelligence.

in india, counter intelligence is being done by the institution called intelligence bureau (ib). similarly, collection of information from foreign countries is undertaken by another institution called 'research and analysis wing (raw).

counter intelligence is not only sensitive, but also complicated. spotting out the foreign spies, working in different covers and foiling their plans and deactivating their activities, are the duties of counter intelligence. deep dedication and continuous effort are required for success, in counter intelligence.

1. take steps, to reduce scope for foreign intelligence agencies, to continue their activities. 2. spotting out the presence of enemy intelligence agencies. 3. destroying the enemy intelligence agencies, after collecting information about them. 4. review of the activities and methods of enemy intelligence agencies and deactivating them.

cultivation of source: reliable and intelligent persons must be selected, in different areas, to get information, about the movements of terrorists. these persons are called, source or informants. these persons, will give information, for the sake of money and to take revenge and at certain times, being patriots and responsible citizens. valuable information can be obtained from these persons. steps should be taken, not to reveal the identity of these persons, otherwise their life will be endangered. the promised remuneration should be promptly paid to the informants.

use of latest technical equipment, in the collection of information: vhf sets, cell phones, mobile tracking system, gps and internet, are being heavily used, for collection of information, in the recent times. special arrangements have been made for this, in the intelligence branch.

techniques of collecting terrorist intelligence: sources and informers, have to be arranged, for collection of information, about the terrorist movements. agents should be secretly sent into terrorist organizations, to collect information, under the pretext of doing a job, in different guises. they should have the skill and technique to escape, with their skin intact, at the time of danger. the intelligence agencies, shall make use of latest technology, to record the conversation of terrorists and in tracing their movements and hide outs.

surveillance on movement of foreigners, drug peddlers and smugglers: the number of foreign visitors to india, is increasing day by day. this may be due to the liberal immigration policies. in these circumstances, the police department must have proper understanding of their duties and powers, in dealing with foreigners. identifying the forged documents and watching the movements of unwanted and suspicious foreigners require a lot of skill for a police officer.

immigration authorities scrutinize the documents like passport and visa to identify the foreigners, who have entered illegally. district special branch will discharge the duties of checking the activities of foreigners, at the district level. in view of the infiltration of terrorists, into the country, the above agencies shall be careful in discharging their duties.
drug peddlers, smugglers, should be booked under the relevant acts, to stop their illegal activities.

page_content: coordination between intelligence wings and organizations: the indian government has established 'multi-agency coordination center, mcc in the year 2006, for coordination between central and state intelligence agencies. this will work for coordination among the institutions, like central intelligence bureau, national technical research organization (n.t.r.o), directorate of revenue intelligence (dri), research and analysis wing (raw), border security force (bsf), military intelligence and various intelligence agencies of states. efforts will be made by this organization, for mutual cooperation and development also. national security council, will also work, with the same motto.

- a. eco terrorism : this concept was started in the year 1970. in usa, some organizations like animal liberation front (alf), earth liberation front (elf), are carrying out attacks, under the guise of protecting the environment. protection of animals and environment, is their chief aim. for this purpose, these organizations do the acts of obstructing the activities of industrial companies and their employees, arson, bombings and freeing the animals, kept in laboratories. these sort of activities, are called as eco-terrorism. but, according to the leaders and members of these organizations, private companies are doing eco terrorism, by torturing animals and spoiling environment, in the name of development. - b. narco terrorism : cultivation of narcotic drugs and substances and their transport, is being undertaken, by the terrorist groups for financing their activities and making the youth as slaves to narcotic substances and drugs. this is called, narco terrorism. in afghanistan, talibans undertake the cultivation of hashish, manufacture heroin, cocaine and morphin, smuggle them and earn money. the money earned in this way is being used for terrorist activities. - c. bio terrorism : use of chemicals and bios by the terrorists, to attack the targets, is called bio terrorism. ex: destroying the crops, killing the animals and human beings by spreading the micro organisms. in america and some other countries, terrorists used anthrax powder. - d. cyber terrorism : logging into the computer system secretly, collection of information, damage of software and conversation in coded language through the internet, by terrorists, will come under cyber terrorism. alqaeda made maximum use of the internet in planning the attacks and conveying information. - e. economic terrorism: circulation of fake currency and investment in stock exchanges and carrying out hawala business, depositing money in secret accounts and using them for their activities by terrorists, is called economic terrorism. intelligence reports prevail, that the terrorists made investments in the bombay stock exchange (bse).

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: role of governments & enforcement of law

page_content: in the post 9/11 world, the threat that terrorism poses has gained an international character. all the nations have seemed to realise the increased vulnerability that has been created to their security system. in such a scenario, the national governments have been pushed to take a front step in order to prevent the ever-looming danger of terrorist activities from growing beyond.

various laws and acts have been passed by the government to combat terrorism. often these laws have been a matter of debate and controversy. some of these legislative measures that have existed or exist today, are:

1. unlawful activities (prevention) act, 1967: the uapa of 1967 allows the law enforcement agencies to act against individuals as well as organisations engaged in unlawful activities, inclusive of terrorism. 2. terrorist and disruptive activities (prevention) act, 1987: the tada act provides stringent measures that deal specifically with terrorism. the act, surrounded by much controversy, ultimately lapsed in 1995. 3. prevention of terrorism act, 2002: as terrorism increased, another law pota, 2002 was passed. this provided law enforcement agencies with even more extensive powers. it was repealed in 2004 by upa after concerns were raised regarding human rights violations. 4. the national investigation agency act, 2008: the nia was established in 2008 to allow the setup of nia at the central level that deals with counter terrorism activities. 5. the anti-hijacking act, 2016: recently, the anti-hijacking act was passed to prevent the hijack of aircraft.

intelligence gathering is a manifestation of such an attempt on the government's part to prevent the spread of terrorism in their territory. the sophisticated technologies have made it possible to track and monitor any suspicious activity and this has saved innumerable tragic events from taking place. yet, one cannot ignore that similar upgraded technologies are used by the terrorist camps to unleash their agendas.

various measures are taken by the national governments to counter terrorism . this includes creating a rigid security environment, constant monitoring, or heightening security at public places & events. strict procedures are undertaken to ensure there is no transportation of any threatening material from one place to another. these procedures are only necessary but not enough in themselves alone.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: major law enforcement agencies of india, undertaking counter-terrorism operations:

page_content: - 1- national investigation agency : the first and foremost agency working at the central level to counter terrorism is the nia. established in 2009, in accordance with the nia act of 2008, it undertakes investigations in relation to terrorism that might pose national implications. nia works in close coordination with other agencies at the central and state levels.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: major law enforcement agencies of india, undertaking counter-terrorism operations:

page_content: - 2- intelligence bureau: ib is the intelligence agency of india. the function of ib is to gather and analyse any intelligence in relation to terrorism that is provided to the law enforcement agencies. the activities performed by various counter terrorism agencies in regard to internal matters is largely based on the analysis of ib. - 3- research and analysis wing : raw is also another intelligence agency. while ib deals with matters internal to the country, the focus of raw is to gather intelligence in relation to any external threats. for this, raw employs a variety of measures, including constant monitoring of the cross-border movements, or working in cooperation with foreign agencies. raw was established in 1968. - 4- police forces : police play an active and important role in counter terrorism. at the level of states, there are various special task forces (stfs) that carry out operations at the state level. at the level of the centre, various paramilitary forces like crpf, bsf, and cisf work to provide security. the work done by various forces are in close coordination with one another. - 5- finance intelligence unit : the fiu-ind is a central agency that is placed under the ministry of finance. while the unit plays various functions of collecting information in regard to suspicious financial transactions, it also traces the cases of money laundering and the sources of terrorist organisations.

page_content: one is well aware that the threat of terrorism has a global face. when such is the case, international cooperation becomes necessary to counter a global problem. as a result, the governments try to create & adhere to mutual agreements that are helpful to combat against terrorism. as of now, there is no international convention that supervises the sharing of vital information like that of intelligence and evidence between countries. a gap also needs to be fulfilled that can allow governments to mutually cooperate in extradition of suspects who have managed to escape in another country. in the same direction, india has submitted the comprehensive convention on international terrorism in the un general assembly. if ratified, it would serve as a basis making all acts of terrorism as illegal.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: the strategies suggested by the united nations on global counterterrrorism:

page_content: the united nations global counter-terrorism strategy (a/res/60/288) is a unique global instrument to enhance national, regional and international efforts to counter terrorism. through its adoption by consensus in 2006, all united nations member states agreed to a common strategic and operational approach to fighting terrorism.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: the strategies suggested by the united nations on global counterterrrorism:

page_content: the strategy reaffirms that member states have the primary responsibility to implement the united nations global counter-terrorism strategy and in preventing and countering terrorism and violent extremism conducive to terrorism. it sends a clear message that terrorism is unacceptable in all its forms and manifestations, and that member states have resolved to take practical steps, individually and collectively, to prevent and combat terrorism. those practical steps include a wide array of measures ranging from strengthening member states' capacity to counter terrorist threats to better coordinating the united nations system's counterterrorism architecture and activities.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: pillars of the united nations global counter-terrorism strategy

page_content: the united nations global counter-terrorism strategy in the form of a resolution and an annexed plan of action (a/res/60/288) is composed of four pillars, namely:

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: pillars of the united nations global counter-terrorism strategy

page_content: 1. measures to address the conditions conducive to the spread of terrorism. 2. measures to prevent and combat terrorism. 3. measures to build states' capacity to prevent and combat terrorism and to strengthen the role of the united nations system in that regard. 4. measures to ensure respect for human rights for all and the rule of law as the fundamental basis of the fight against terrorism.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: the indian government has taken various measures to prevent terrorism :

page_content: through means of legislation, it has enacted and amended laws to meet the challenge against terrorism. for instance, the unlawful activities act of 1967 was recently amended in 2019. this was done to furnish more powers to the agencies concerned with security. the government of india also collaborates and cooperates with the special agencies of different countries. we have cooperation with intelligence agencies of the usa, israel and others that help to swap information related to terrorism or intelligence. the indous counter terrorism initiative was first discussed in 2009 that not only enhances the relationship between the two nations but also provides the opportunity to strengthen its capabilities by exchanging information in regard to technology, best practices, and their expertise. in 2022, the 19th meeting of the counter terrorism joint working group was conducted. the government of india has also initiated counter terrorism activities with the neighbouring countries of our south asian subsystem like bangladesh, sri lanka, to name a few.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: role of police in counter terrorism

page_content: the role of the police in counter-terrorism is crucial, given the persistent threat of terrorism in the region. the police play a multifaceted role in addressing and preventing acts of terrorism. here are key aspects of their role:

surveillance: police engage in monitoring and surveillance activities to gather intelligence on potential terrorist threats. this involves tracking individuals, monitoring communications, and analyzing information to identify potential risks.

- community policing: building strong relationships with communities is vital for gathering intelligence and preventing radicalization. community policing helps in identifying and addressing potential threats at an early stage.
- public awareness: police conduct awareness campaigns to educate the public about the signs of radicalization and the importance of reporting suspicious activities.

anti-terrorism squads (ats): many states in india have specialized ats units within the police force. these units are trained to handle counter-terrorism operations, investigate terror-related cases, and respond swiftly to potential threats.

special weapons and tactics (swat) teams: police maintain swat teams capable of rapid response to terrorist incidents. these teams are equipped and trained to handle hostage situations, bombings, and other high-risk scenarios.

- counter-terrorism units: police investigate and analyze terrorist activities through specialized units dedicated to counter-terrorism. this involves collecting evidence, conducting raids, and collaborating with other intelligence agencies. - forensic expertise: utilizing forensic techniques to gather and analyze evidence is a crucial aspect of police investigations into terrorist activities.

enforcement of anti-terrorism laws: the police are responsible for enforcing laws related to counter-terrorism. this includes taking legal actions against individuals involved in terrorist activities and ensuring the adherence to legal processes.

collaboration with international agencies: indian police collaborate with international law enforcement agencies to share intelligence and coordinate efforts to combat terrorism with a global perspective.

- critical infrastructure protection: the police work to secure critical infrastructure, transportation hubs, and other potential targets to minimize the risk of terrorist attacks. - border security: given india's geopolitical context, the police are involved in securing borders to prevent illegal activities, including the infiltration of terrorists.

training programs: regular training programs are conducted for police personnel to enhance their skills in dealing with terrorism. this includes training in the latest technologies, intelligence gathering techniques, and counter-terrorism strategies.

cyber crime units: with the growing threat of cyber-terrorism, police in india also focus on building capabilities to address cyber threats. cybercrime units play a crucial role in monitoring online activities related to terrorism.

the effective coordination of these efforts, along with collaboration between state and central agencies, is essential for a comprehensive and successful counter-terrorism strategy in india. the police's role extends beyond reactive measures to include proactive strategies aimed at preventing and mitigating the impact of terrorist activities.

to take up the challenge of terrorism in possible infested areas of possible terrorism/ leftwing extremism, is an important responsibility of the police as well as the people. the government machinery including police have to understand the reasons of terrorism, which may be left-wing terrorism or islamic terrorism which have to be nipped off in the bud and stern actions have to be taken so that it won't brew into bigger acts of terror whether it's in the jurisdiction or anywhere in the country. the people in the area have to be sensitised against the radicalisation either towards leftwing extremism or towards fundamental terrorism. the community leaders have to be taken into confidence to pass on any information relating to slightest suspicious activities of terrorism .the recruitment of cadres, if any, into their organisations have to be dealt with seriously, the suspicious elements have to be kept under proper surveillance. the radicalisation of youth has to be prevented by taking legal action against the fanatic ideologists. the deradicalisation has to be done with the help of specialised agencies such as nia etc. every information related to terrorist activities has to be shared with the concerned institutions dealing with terrorism for coordinated efforts to foil any possible terroristic activity or attacks.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: arms act, 1959 act no. 54 of 1959 [23 rd december , 1959.]

page_content: be it enacted by parliament in the tenth year of the republic of india as follows:-

- (1) this act may be called the arms act, 1959. - (2) it extends to the whole of india. - (3) it shall come into force on such date as the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, appoint.
(1) in this act, unless the context otherwise requires,-

- (a) 'acquisition', with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes hiring, borrowing, or accepting as a gift; - (b) 'ammunition' means ammunition for any firearm, and includes- - (i) rockets, bombs, grenades, shells [and other missiles,] - (ii) article designed for torpedo service and submarine mining, - (iii) other articles containing, or designed or adapted to contain, explosive, fulminating or fissionable material or noxious liquid, gas or other such thing, whether capable of use with firearms or not, - (iv) charges for firearms and accessories for such charges, - (v) fuses and friction tubes, - (vi) parts of, and machinery for manufacturing, ammunition, and - (vii) such ingredients of ammunition as the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, specify in this behalf; - (c) 'arms' means articles of any description designed or adapted as weapons for offence or defence, and includes firearms, sharpedged and other deadly weapons, and parts of, and machinery for manufacturing, arms, but does not include articles designed solely for domestic or agricultural uses such as a lathi or an ordinary walking stick and weapons incapable of being used otherwise than as toys or of being converted into serviceable weapons;

- (d) 'district magistrate', in relation to any area for which a commissioner of police has been appointed, means the commissioner of police thereof and includes any such deputy commissioner of police, exercising jurisdiction over the whole or any part of such area, as may be specified by the state government in this behalf in relation to such area or part;] - (e) 'firearms' means arms of any description designed or adapted to discharge a projectile or projectiles of any kind by the action of any explosive or other forms of energy, and includes- - (i) artillery, hand-grenades, riot-pistols or weapons of any kind designed or adapted for the discharge of any noxious liquid, gas or other such thing, - (ii) accessories for any such firearm designed or adapted to diminish the noise or flash caused by the firing thereof, - (iii) parts of, and machinery for manufacturing, firearms, and - (iv) carriages, platforms and appliances for mounting, transporting and serving artillery; - 'licence' means a licence issued in accordance with the provisions of this act and rules made there under and includes a licence issued in the electronic form;] - (f) 'licensing authority' means an officer or authority empowered to grant or renew licences under rules made under this act, and includes the government; - 'magistrate' means an executive magistrate under the code of criminal procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974);] - (g) 'prescribed' means prescribed by rules made under this act; - (h) 'prohibited ammunition' means any ammunition containing, or designed or adapted to contain, any noxious liquid, gas or other such thing, and includes rockets, bombs, grenades, shells, [missiles,] articles designed for torpedo service and submarine mining and such other articles as the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, specify to be prohibited ammunition; - (i) 'prohibited arms' means- - (i) firearms so designed or adapted that, if pressure is applied to the trigger, missiles continue to be discharged until pressure is removed from the trigger or the magazine containing the missiles is empty, or - (ii) weapons of any description designed or adapted for the discharge

of any noxious liquid, gas or other such thing, and includes artillery, anti-aircraft and anti-tank firearms and such other arms as the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, specify to be prohibited arms;

- (j) 'public servant' has the same meaning as in section 21 of the indian penal code (45 of 1860); - (2) 'transfer' with its grammatical variations and cognate expressions, includes letting on hire, lending, giving and parting with possession. - (3) for the purposes of this act, the length of the barrel of a firearm shall be measured from the muzzle to the point at which the charge is exploded on firing. - (4) any reference in this act to any law which is not in force in any area shall, in relation to that area, be construed as a reference to the corresponding law, if any, in force in that area. - (5) any reference in this act to any officer or authority shall, in relation to any area in which there is not officer or authority with the same designation, be construed as a reference to such officer or authority as may be specified by the central government by notification in the official gazette.

time and in such manner as may be by rule prescribed give notice thereof and of the attendant loss of human life or personal injury, if any, to the 3 [chief controller of explosives] and] to the officer in charge of the nearest police-station.

- ( 1 ) where any accident such as is referred to in section 8 occurs in or about or in connection with [any place, aircraft, carriage or vessel] under the control of any of [armed forces of the union], an inquiry into the causes of the accident shall be held by the naval, military, or air force authority concerned, and where any such accident occurs in any other circumstances, the district magistrate shall, in cases attended by loss of human life, or may, in any other case, hold or direct a magistrate subordinate to him to hold, such an inquiry.. - (2) any person holding an inquiry under this section shall have all the powers of a magistrate in holding an inquiry into an offence under the [code of criminal procedure, 1973 (2 of 1974)], and may exercise such of the powers conferred on any officer by rules under section 7 as he may think it necessary or expedient to exercise for the purposes of the inquiry. - (3) the person holding an inquiry under this section shall make a report to the central government stating the causes of the accident and its circumstances. - (4) the central government may make rules- - (a) to regulate the procedure at inquiries under this section; - (b) to enable the [chief controller of explosives] to be present or represented at any such inquiry; - (c) to permit the [chief controller of explosives] or his representative to examine any witnesses at the inquiry; - (d) to provide that where the [chief controller of explosives] is not present or represented at any such inquiry, a report of the proceedings thereof shall be sent to him; - (e) to prescribe the manner in which and the time within which notices referred to in section 8 shall be given.

- ( 1 ) the central government may, where it is of opinion, whether or not it has received the report of an inquiry under section 9, that an inquiry or more formal character should be held into the causes of an accident such as is referred to in section 8, appoint the [chief controller of explosives] or
any other competent person to hold such inquiry, and may also appoint one or more persons possessing legal or special knowledge to act as assessors in such inquiry.

- (2) where the central government orders an inquiry under this section, it may also direct that any inquiry under section 9 pending at the time shall be discontinued. - (3) the person appointed to hold an inquiry under this section shall have all the powers of a civil court under the code of civil procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908), for the purposes of enforcing the attendance of witnesses and compelling the production of documents and material objects; and every person required by such person as aforesaid to furnish any information shall be deemed to be legally bound so to do within the meaning of section 176 of the indian penal code (45 of 1890). - (4) any person holding an inquiry under this section may exercise such of the powers conferred on any officer by rules under section 7 as he may think it necessary or expedient to exercise for the purposes of the inquiry. - (5) the person holding an inquiry under this section shall make a report to the central government stating the causes of the accident and its circumstances, and adding any observations which he or any of the assessors may think fit to make; and the central government shall cause every report so made to be published at such time and in such manner as it may think fit. - (6) the central government may make rules for regulating the procedure at inquiries under this section]

fine which may extend to five thousand rupees or with both; and in the case of importation by water, the owner and master of the vessel or in the ease of importation by air, the owner, and the master of the aircraft, in which the explosive is imported shall, in the absence of reasonable excuse, each be punishable with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.

- (3) whoever,- 2. (a) manufactures, sells, transports, imports, exports or possesses any explosive in contravention of the provisions of clause (a) of section 6a; or 3. (b) sells, delivers or despatches any explosive in contravention of the provisions of clause ( b ) of that section, shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine or with both; or 4. - ( 1 ) whenever an offence under this act has been committed by a company, every person who at the time the offence was committed was in charge of, or was responsible t6 the company for the conduct of the business of the company, as well as the company, shall be deemed to be guilty of the offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly:

provided that nothing contained in this sub-section shall render any such person liable to any punishment under this act if he proves that the offence was committed without his knowledge and that he exercised all due diligence to prevent the commission of such offence.

- (2) notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section ( 1 ) , where an offence under this act has been committed by a company and it is proved that the offence has been committed with the consent or connivance of, or is attributable to any neglect on the part of, any director, manager, secretary or other officer of the company, such director, manager, secretary or other officer shall also be deemed to be guilty of that offence and shall be liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly.
explanation.- for the purposes of this section,-

- (a) 'company' means anybody corporate, and includes a firm or other association of individuals;and - (b) 'director', in relation to a firm, means a partner in the firm.] 10. forfeiture of explosives.when a person is convicted of an offence punishable under this act, or the rules made under this act, the court before which he is convicted may direct that the explosive, or ingredient of the explosive, or the substance (if any) in respect of which the offence has been committed, or any part of that explosive, ingredient or substance, shall, with the receptacles containing the same, be forfeited. 11. distress of aircraft or vessel.where the owner or master of any aircraft or vessel is adjudged under this act to pay a fine for any offence committed with, or in relation to, that aircraft or vessel, the. court may, in addition to any power it may have for the purpose of compelling payment of the fine, direct it to be levied by distress and sale of,- - (a) the aircraft and its furniture or so much of the furniture, or - (b) the vessel and the tackle, apparel and furniture of such vessel or so much of the tackle, apparel and furniture thereof, as is necessary for the payment of the fine.] 12. abetment and attempts.whoever abets, within the meaning of the indian penal code (45 of 1860), the commission of an offence punishable under this act, or the rules made under this act, or attempts to commit any such offence and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the same, shall be punished as if he had committed the offence. 13. power to arrest without warrant persons committing dangerous offences.whoever is found committing any act for which he is punishable under this act or the rules under this act, and which tends to cause explosion or fire in or about any place where an explosive is manufactured or stored, or any railway or port, or any carriage, [aircraft or vessel], may be apprehended without a warrant by a police-officer, or by the occupier of, or the agent or servant of, or other person authorized by the occupier of, that place, or by any agent or servant of, or other person authorized by, the railway administration or 3 [conservator of the port or officer in charge of the air port], and be removed from the place where he is arrested and conveyed as soon as conveniently may be before a magistrate.
- ( 1 ) nothing in this act, except sections 8, 9 and 9a shall apply to the manufacture, possession, use, transport or importation of any explosive-

- (a) by any of the 5 [armed forces of the union, and ordnance factories or other establishments of such forces) in accordance with rules or regulations made by the central government; - (b) by any person employed under [the central government or under a state government] in execution of this act. ( 2 ) the central government may by notification in the official gazette exempt, absolutely or subject to any such conditions as it may think fit to impose, [any explosive and any person or class of persons from all or any of the provisions of this act or the rules made there under.] 15. saving of arms act, 1959.nothing in this act shall affect the provisions of the [arms act, 1959 (4 of 1959)]:

provided that an authority granting a license under this act for the manufacture, possession, sale, transport or importation of an explosive may, if empowered in this behalf by the rules under which the license is granted, direct by an order written on the license that it shall have the effect of a like license granted under the said arms ( 4 of 1959) act, 1959.

16. saving as to liability under other law.nothing in this act or the rules under this act shall prevent any person from being prosecuted under any other law for any act or omission which constitutes an offence against this act or those rules, or from being l iable under that other law to any other or higher punishment or penalty than that provided by this act or those rules:
provided that a person shall not be punished twice for the same offence.

17. extension of definition of 'explosive' to other explosive substances.the central government may, from time to time, by notification in the official gazette, declare that any substance which appears to the central government to be specially dangerous to life or property, by reason either of its explosive properties or of any process in the manufacture thereof being liable to explosion, shall be deemed to be an explosive within the meaning of this act; and the provisions of this act (subject to such exceptions, limitations and restrictions as may be specified in the notification) shall accordingly extend to that substance in like manner as if it were included in the definition of the term 'explosive' in this act. 2. 17a. power to delegate.the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, direct that any power or function which may be exercised or performed by i t under this act other than the power under sections 5, 6, 6a, 14 and 17 may, in relation to such matters and subject to such conditions, if any, as it may specify in the
notification, be exercised or performed also by-
- (a) such officer or authority subordinate to the central government, or - (b) such state government or such officer or authority subordinate to the state government.]

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: procedure for making, publication and confirmation of rules.-

page_content: - ( 1 ) an authority making rules under this act shall before making the rules, publish a draft of the proposed rules for the information of persons likely to be affected thereby. - (2) the publication shall be made in such manner as the central government, from time to time, by notification in the official gazette prescribes. - (3) there shall be published with the draft a notice specifying a date at or after which the draft will be taken into consideration. - (4) the authority making the rules shall receive and consider any objection or suggestion which may be made by any person with respect to the draft before the date so specified. a rule made under this act shall not take effect until it has been published in the official gazette - (5) the publication in the official gazette of a rule purporting to be made under this act shall be conclusive evidence that it has been duly made, and, if it, requires sanction, that it has been duly sanctioned. - (6) all powers to make rules conferred by this act may he exercised from time to time as occasion requires. - (7) every rule made under this act shall be laid, as soon as may be after it is made, before each house of parliament, while it is in session, for a total period of thirty days which may be comprised in one session or in two or more successive sessions, and if, before the expiry of the session immediately following the session or the successive sessions aforesaid, both houses agree in making any modification in the rule or both houses agree that the rule should not be made, the rule shall thereafter have effect only in such modified form or be of no effect, as the case may be; so, however, that any such modification or annulment shall be without prejudice to the validity of anything previously done under that rule.]

page_content: whereas it is necessary further to amend the law relating to explosive substances; it is hereby enacted as follows:-
- ) this act may be called the explosive substances act, 1908. - ( 1 - ( 2 ) it extends to the whole of india and applies also to citizens of india [outside india].]

- the expression 'explosive substance' shall be deemed to include any materials for making any explosive substance; also any apparatus, machine, implement or material used, or intended to be used, or adapted for causing, or aiding in causing, any explosion in or with any explosive substance; also any part of any such apparatus, - (a) machine or implement; - (b) the expression 'special category explosive substance' shall be deemed to include research development explosive (rdx), penta erythritol tetra nitrate (petn), high melting explosive (hmx), tri nitro toluene (tnt), low temperature plastic explosive (ltpe), composition exploding (ce) (2, 4, 6 phenyl methyl nitramine or tetryl), octol (mixture of high melting explosive and tri nitro toluene), plastic explosive kirkee -1 (pek-1) and rdx/tnt compounds and other similar type of explosives and a combination thereof and remote control devices causing explosion and any other substance and a combination thereof which the central government may, by notification in the official gazette, specify or the purposes of this act. 3. punishment for causing explosion likely to endanger life or property .-any person who unlawfully and maliciously causes by- - (a) any explosive substance an explosion of a nature likely to endanger life or to cause serious injury to property shall, whether any injury to person or property has been actually caused or not, be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment of either description which shall not be less than (ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; - (b) - any special category explosive substance an explosion of a nature

likely to endanger life or to cause serious injury to property shall, whether any injury to person or property has been actually caused or not, be punished with death, or rigorous imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

4. punishment for attempt to cause explosion, or for making or keeping explosive with intent to endanger life or property. -any person who unlawfully and maliciously2. (a) does any act with intent to cause by an explosive substance or special category explosive substance, or conspires lo cause by an explosive substance or special category explosive substance, an explosion of a nature likely to endanger life or to cause serious injury to property; or makes or has in his possession or under his control any explosive substance or special category explosive substance with intent by means thereof to endanger life, or cause serious injury to property, or to enable any other person by means thereof to endanger life or cause serious injury to property in india, shall, whether any explosion does or does not take place and whether any injury to person or property has been actually caused or not, be punished,- 3. (i) in the case of any explosive substance, with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; 4. (ii) in the case of any special category explosive substance, with rigorous imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. 5. punishment for making or possessing explosives under suspicious circumstances. -any person who makes or knowingly has in his possession or under his control any explosive substance or special category explosive substance, under such circumstances as to give rise to a reasonable suspicion that he is not making it or does not have it in his possession or under his control for a lawful object, shall, unless he can show that he made it or had it in his possession or under his control for a lawful object, be punished,- 6. (a) in the case of any explosive substance, with imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; 7. (b) in the case of any special category explosive substance, with rigorous imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.] 6. punishment of abettors.any person who by the supply or solicitation for money, the providing of premises, the supply of materials, or in any
manner whatsoever, procures, counsels, aids, abets, or is accessory to, the commission of any offence under this act shall be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.
7. restriction on trial of offences.no court shall proceed to the trial of any person for an offence against this act except with the consent of the [district magistrate].

page_content: whoever wages war against the government of india, or attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life] [and shall also be liable to fine.

metadata.subject: public_order_ethics
metadata.heading: 121a. conspiracy to commit offences punishable by section 121 . -

page_content: whoever within or without [india] conspires to commit any of the offences punishable by section 121, or conspires to overawe, by means of criminal force or the show of criminal force, [the central government or any [state] government, shall be punished with [imprisonment for life], or with imprisonment of either description which may extend to ten years, [and shall also be liable to fine].
